question: [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to supply collateral on behalf of the taker for a collateral index that has a reverting oracle, causing Midnight.take to revert with an oracle revert when checking the seller's health (since the seller has non-zero debt and an activated collateral with a reverting oracle), which is caught by try/catch, all offers are skipped, and OutOfOffers() reverts, under preconditions where the market has a collateral with an oracle that can be made to revert and the attacker can supply that collateral on behalf of the taker? Call sequence: attacker calls supplyCollateral(market, badOracleIndex, 1, taker); victim's bundle executes; take reverts due to oracle revert in health check; all offers skipped; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: oracle reverts must not be exploitable to block bundle operations. Scoped impact: DoS against sell-side take via MidnightBundles. Proof idea: state test where a collateral oracle is made to revert after being activated for the taker.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: repayAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS against a victim's repay bundle by front-running to repay the victim's entire debt (via Midnight.repay(market, victimDebt, victim, ...)), causing the victim's subsequent repayAndWithdrawCollateral call to repay 0 units of actual debt (since debt is already 0), but the collateral withdrawal then fails if the victim's position is unhealthy (which it shouldn't be with 0 debt), OR the repay succeeds as a no-op but the referral fee is still charged, under preconditions where the attacker has enough loan tokens to repay the victim's debt? Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, victimDebt, victim, ...); victim's repayAndWithdrawCollateral executes; repay is a no-op (debt already 0); referral fee is still deducted from victim's assets; victim loses referralFeeAssets. Invariant tested: repayAndWithdrawCollateral must not charge referral fees when the underlying repay is a no-op. Scoped impact: fund loss (referral fee) and potential DoS against repay via MidnightBundles. Proof idea: state test where victim's debt is repaid before bundle call, assert referral fee is not charged.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by crafting a takes[] array where takes[0].offer.market is a different market than the one the victim intends to use, causing touchMarket at line 64 to create/touch the wrong market, and all subsequent InconsistentMarket checks at line 73 to fail for the actual intended offers (since id is derived from takes[0].offer.market), reverting the bundle with InconsistentMarket(), under preconditions where the attacker is the caller (msg.sender == taker) or an authorized operator? Call sequence: attacker calls bundle with takes[0].offer.market = wrongMarket and takes[1..n].offer.market = correctMarket; touchMarket creates wrongMarket; InconsistentMarket() reverts for takes[1]. Invariant tested: bundle functions must validate that all takes reference the same market as takes[0]. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles through market inconsistency. Proof idea: unit test with mixed markets in takes[], assert InconsistentMarket() revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to trigger a flash loan that temporarily drains the market's withdrawable balance, causing Midnight.take to succeed but the subsequent withdrawCollateral at lines 92-99 to revert because the market has insufficient withdrawable assets, reverting the entire bundle, under preconditions where the victim's bundle includes collateral withdrawals and the market's withdrawable balance is near zero? Call sequence: attacker takes a flash loan draining withdrawable; victim's bundle executes; takes succeed; withdrawCollateral reverts due to insufficient withdrawable; entire bundle reverts. Invariant tested: collateral withdrawal after successful takes must not be blockable by flash loan manipulation of withdrawable balance. Scoped impact: DoS against take-and-withdraw bundle via MidnightBundles. Proof idea: state test where withdrawable is drained before bundle call.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to supply a dust amount of a fee-on-transfer collateral token on behalf of the taker, causing Midnight.supplyCollateral at line 139 to record more collateral than was actually received (due to fee-on-transfer), and then the subsequent take to revert due to accounting inconsistency, under preconditions where the market accepts a fee-on-transfer collateral token and the attacker can supply it on behalf of the taker? Call sequence: attacker calls supplyCollateral(market, feeOnTransferIndex, dustAmount, taker); Midnight records dustAmount but receives less; victim's bundle executes; take reverts due to health check inconsistency; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: fee-on-transfer collateral tokens must not corrupt accounting in bundle operations. Scoped impact: DoS against sell-side bundle via MidnightBundles with fee-on-transfer collateral. Proof idea: state test with fee-on-transfer collateral token, assert accounting inconsistency.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to change the market's continuous fee (via Midnight.setMarketContinuousFee) to MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE, causing the buyer's pendingFee to increase dramatically in the take, potentially causing toUint128 to overflow and revert with CastOverflow(), which is caught by try/catch, all offers are skipped, and OutOfOffers() reverts, under preconditions where the attacker is the feeSetter and the buyer has a large credit position with long time to maturity? Call sequence: feeSetter calls setMarketContinuousFee(id, MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE); victim's bundle executes; take reverts with CastOverflow() in pendingFee computation; all offers skipped; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: continuous fee changes must not cause overflow in bundle take operations. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles through continuous fee manipulation. Proof idea: state test with MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE and large credit position, assert no overflow.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to call Midnight.updatePosition(market, taker) which crystallizes the taker's pending continuous fee, causing the taker's credit to decrease (via lossFactor application), and if the taker's credit drops to 0, the subsequent take may behave differently (e.g., sellerCreditDecrease = 0 instead of expected value), causing the bundle to fill fewer units than expected and OutOfOffers() to revert, under preconditions where the taker has credit and the market has a non-zero lossFactor? Call sequence: attacker calls updatePosition(market, taker); taker's credit is slashed; victim's bundle executes; take fills fewer units than expected; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: position updates must not be exploitable to block bundle operations. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles through forced position update. Proof idea: state test where taker's credit is slashed before bundle call.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by exploiting the interaction between TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits and the settlement fee computation, where the settlement fee is computed using zeroFloorSub(offer.market.maturity, block.timestamp) at line 24 of TakeAmountsLib.sol, but Midnight.take also uses zeroFloorSub(offer.market.maturity, block.timestamp) at line 359, and if the market matures between the TakeAmountsLib call and the Midnight.take call (impossible in same tx), OR if the settlement fee at TTM=0 differs from TTM=1 causing a 1-wei discrepancy in buyerAssets, causing the exact equality check to fail? Call sequence: bundle executes at block.timestamp == maturity - 1; TakeAmountsLib uses TTM=1; Midnight.take uses TTM=1; no discrepancy. Invariant tested: settlement fee computation must be consistent between TakeAmountsLib and Midnight.take within the same transaction. Scoped impact: DoS against take via buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral at maturity boundary. Proof idea: fuzz over block.timestamp near maturity and assert filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to supply collateral on behalf of the taker for a collateral index that is not in the market's collateralParams, causing Midnight.supplyCollateral at line 139 to revert (or record to wrong index), reverting the entire bundle before any takes are attempted, under preconditions where the attacker can call supplyCollateral with an out-of-bounds collateralIndex? Call sequence: attacker calls supplyCollateral(market, outOfBoundsIndex, amount, taker); Midnight reverts; victim's bundle reverts. Invariant tested: collateral supply with invalid index must revert cleanly. Scoped impact: DoS against sell-side bundle via MidnightBundles. Proof idea: unit test with out-of-bounds collateralIndex in collateralSupplies, assert revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by crafting a takes[] array where all offers have offer.buy = true (buy offers) but the function requires !takes[i].offer.buy (sell offers), causing the InconsistentSide() revert at line 72 for the first offer, reverting the entire bundle before any takes are attempted, under preconditions where the caller is the taker or an authorized operator? Call sequence: caller invokes buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with takes[0].offer.buy = true; InconsistentSide() reverts. Invariant tested: bundle functions must validate offer side before attempting takes. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles through side inconsistency. Proof idea: unit test with buy offers in buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral, assert InconsistentSide() revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(bundler, false, taker) (revoking the bundler's authorization for the taker), causing the authorization check at line 60 to fail with Unauthorized(), reverting the entire bundle, under preconditions where the taker has previously authorized the bundler and the attacker is the taker or an authorized operator of the taker? Call sequence: attacker calls setIsAuthorized(bundler, false, taker); victim's bundle executes; isAuthorized(taker, bundler) returns false; Unauthorized() reverts. Invariant tested: authorization revocation must not be exploitable as a DoS vector by a third party. Scoped impact: DoS against all bundle operations via MidnightBundles. Proof idea: state test where taker's authorization of bundler is revoked before bundle call.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to call Midnight.take with the same offer but with a different taker (the attacker), consuming exactly the units needed to reduce consumableUnits to just below the per-offer slice needed by the victim, causing the victim's bundle to fill fewer units than targetUnits and OutOfOffers() to revert, under preconditions where the attacker has enough loan tokens and the offer has a shared consumption group with other offers? Call sequence: attacker calls take(offer, ..., partialUnits, attacker, ...); consumableUnits for victim's bundle decreases; victim's bundle executes; filledUnits < targetUnits; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: partial consumption by a third party must not permanently block a victim's bundle. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles at low attacker cost. Proof idea: fuzz over partialUnits and assert victim's bundle reverts when consumableUnits < needed.
